internal_forecasts.label: FY2025 Total Cloud Revenue
risk_flags: OCI capacity additions gated by GPU chipmaker delivery schedules
Enterprise SaaS renewal cycle slower than modeled in H2
internal_forecasts.note: Internal FP&A base case entering the second half of FY2025 was $24.2B. The $25B floor Safra Catz committed to publicly required full delivery of planned OCI capacity additions and no material slippage in large SaaS renewals. Gap closed relative to prior quarter; the call was made with moderate confidence that execution would bridge it.

internal_forecasts.label: OCI Annualized Revenue Run Rate
internal_forecasts.note: Infrastructure revenue trajectory was the clearest signal heading into Q2. Internal model had $9.8B annualized; actual Q2 IaaS results supported the $10B+ run-rate claim. High confidence at time of disclosure.

known_risks_at_disclosure: GPU delivery from NVIDIA compressed OCI build timeline
Large enterprise SaaS bookings softer than FY2025 plan in Q1
commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The $25B commitment was $0.8B above the FP&A base case — a meaningful stretch at the time. Management had visibility into a strong OCI pipeline but capacity construction was behind schedule. Commitment served as a motivational floor rather than a high-confidence forecast. Actual FY2025 cloud revenue came in at $24.5B, missing the public floor by $0.5B.

internal_forecasts.label: FY2026 Total Revenue
risk_flags: CapEx ramp to $25B+ requires flawless hyperscaler contract execution
Revenue recognition timing on sovereign AI deals uncertain
Currency headwinds not fully offset in plan
internal_forecasts.note: The $67B FY2026 target announced at the FY2025 year-end call was $4.5B above the internal base case of $62.5B. FP&A modeled three scenarios: base ($62.5B), upside with full contract execution ($67B), and downside with CapEx delays ($59B). Management selected the upside scenario as the public commitment. This is the widest internal-to-public gap in the Oracle dataset and reflects board-level aspiration rather than FP&A consensus.

internal_forecasts.label: OCI Revenue Growth FY2026
risk_flags: Hyperscaler contract capacity delivery is the binding constraint
Competing OCI regions behind plan going into FY2026
internal_forecasts.note: Larry Ellison's public guidance of >70% OCI growth for FY2026 was 12 percentage points above the internal long-range plan at 58%. The internal model assumed capacity additions lagging public commitments by one to two quarters. The 70% figure assumed aggressive bookings-to-revenue conversion on the massive sovereign AI pipeline.

internal_forecasts.label: FY2026 Capital Expenditures
risk_flags: Data center construction timelines subject to permitting delays
internal_forecasts.note: The FY2026 CapEx guidance of >$25B at the Q4 FY2025 call already exceeded the internal plan of $22B. Construction acceleration required to hit the OCI growth targets was not fully reflected in the base plan. This number would be revised upward twice more before year-end.

known_risks_at_disclosure: Internal base case $62.5B vs. public $67B — largest historical gap
RPO recognition timing highly uncertain for multi-year AI contracts
CapEx delivery risk directly gates revenue capacity
commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The $67B commitment required every upside scenario to materialize simultaneously: full CapEx deployment, aggressive RPO-to-revenue conversion, and no macro slowdown. The FP&A team flagged this as aspirational guidance at the time. Management's rationale was that the RPO backlog ($138B at Q4) provided sufficient coverage to justify the number publicly.

known_risks_at_disclosure: RPO doubling requires hyperscaler deals to sign and retain in the period
commitment_assessments.analyst_note: Public commitment to >100% RPO growth in FY2026 (from $138B base) implied an RPO target exceeding $276B. At the time of disclosure, internal conversations with Microsoft, Google, and several sovereign entities were in advanced stages but not signed. The subsequent $317B single-quarter RPO jump in Q1 FY2026 validated the thesis, but the commitment was made before those deals closed.

internal_forecasts.label: OCI Revenue FY2026
risk_flags: Sovereign AI capacity utilization lag between contract and revenue
Multi-cloud integration complexity delaying enterprise adoption
internal_forecasts.note: After the extraordinary Q1 RPO jump to $455B, internal models were revised sharply upward. The OCI FY2026 revenue forecast moved from $13B (pre-Q1 model) to $15.5B as bookings confidence improved. The public $18B commitment assumed faster conversion of the backlog than the internal team believed achievable. Actual Q2 OCI annualized run rate ($4.1B × 4 = $16.4B) suggested trajectory toward the upper end of internal estimates.

internal_forecasts.label: FY2026 CapEx (revised)
risk_flags: Power procurement in key regions constrained
internal_forecasts.note: CapEx guidance was revised to ~$35B at Q1 FY2026, up from the prior $25B floor. Internal FP&A had modeled $32B based on signed contracts and construction milestones. The $3B gap reflected management's confidence in additional hyperscaler commitments closing in Q2.

commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The $455B RPO figure was an actual reported number, not a forward commitment. Internal teams validated the number through contract accounting review. The extraordinary jump ($138B → $455B in one quarter) was driven by a small number of very large multi-year hyperscaler AI infrastructure contracts. This is a retrospective claim with full internal alignment.

known_risks_at_disclosure: Long-range OCI revenue projections ($32B→$73B→$114B→$144B) assume market share not yet won
Competitive landscape for AI infrastructure shifts rapidly
No internal model supports the $144B figure within planning horizon
commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The four-year OCI trajectory ($32B, $73B, $114B, $144B) disclosed by Larry Ellison represents the top-of-range scenario from the strategic planning process, not the FP&A base case. Internal models had the four-year sequence at $28B, $52B, $80B, $105B. The public figures were based on win rates in the current RFP pipeline being sustained over a multi-year horizon — a very aggressive assumption.

internal_forecasts.label: FY2026 Total Revenue (maintained)
risk_flags: CapEx surge to ~$50B (up $15B from Q1 guidance) compresses near-term FCF
Revenue recognition on RPO additions ($523B) back-loaded into FY2027+
internal_forecasts.note: Despite maintaining the $67B public guidance, the internal picture became more complex in Q2. A $15B upward revision to CapEx guidance absorbed significant cash and signaled that capacity was being built well beyond what FY2026 revenue could absorb. Internal revised to $65.5B on tighter H2 linearity assumptions. Management held the public number to preserve analyst confidence.

internal_forecasts.label: FY2026 CapEx (Q2 revision)
internal_forecasts.note: The Q2 CapEx revision to ~$50B ($15B higher than Q1's $35B guidance) was well-supported internally — the incremental capacity commitments were signed contracts. High confidence at disclosure. The free cash flow impact (negative $10B in Q2) was an expected consequence.

known_risks_at_disclosure: RPO additions in Q2 concentrated in 2028+ recognition windows
$4B FY2027 revenue additionality assumes contract ramp timing
commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The $4B incremental FY2027 revenue from Q2 RPO additions was modeled internally as $3.1B base case. The $4B figure assumed favorable timing on hyperscaler capacity utilization ramp. Moderate governance risk: directionally correct but the magnitude depends on factors not fully in Oracle's control.

internal_forecasts.label: Q3 FY2026 Cloud Revenue Growth
risk_flags: Multi-cloud capacity ramp timing uncertain across 45+ regions
Sovereign AI utilization still ramping from signed contracts
internal_forecasts.note: Q3 FY2026 cloud revenue grew 33% year-over-year, slightly below the midpoint of the 40-44% guidance range given in Q2. Internal modeled 38% growth for Q3. The shortfall reflects slower-than-expected ramp of new OCI capacity in several sovereign regions where construction completed later than planned.

commitment_assessments.analyst_note: The 15% TikTok equity stake and board seat were disclosed as factual accomplished events. Internal teams were well-briefed and the statement reflects an already-executed transaction. Full alignment.

The commitment not to issue additional bonds beyond the $50B financing plan was supported by treasury's internal liquidity analysis. Sufficient cash generation from operations was projected to fund ongoing CapEx without additional debt issuance. High confidence, low governance risk.
